does: This paper surveys machine unlearning approaches for large language models, examining methods to permanently remove sensitive information without costly full retraining. It reviews technical solutions including differential privacy, homomorphic encryption, federated learning, and ephemeral memory architectures, alongside evaluation techniques to verify forgetting has occurred and assess resilience against adversarial recovery attacks. The survey catalogs current unlearning mechanisms—both algorithmic approaches and institutional safeguards like auditing and regulatory frameworks—while assessing how well existing solutions address the fundamental challenge of ensuring permanent, verifiable deletion of private data from trained models.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of machine unlearning, which addresses a gap in prior deep learning research that largely ignored data removal after training. It connects to foundational privacy work in differential privacy and federated learning, established techniques for training without retaining sensitive information. The survey extends these classical privacy approaches to the specific challenges of LLMs—their scale, memorization properties, and deployment in sensitive domains. It also engages with recent adversarial robustness literature examining whether removed information can be recovered through attacks, and connects to ongoing debates about transparency, accountability, and regulatory compliance in AI systems.
enables: This survey establishes a foundation for developing more robust unlearning standards and verification protocols for LLMs in high-stakes applications like healthcare and finance. It points toward hybrid technical-institutional approaches where privacy guarantees are strengthened through auditing, formal verification of forgetting, and regulatory oversight. The work enables future research into efficient unlearning mechanisms that balance computational cost with security guarantees, development of adversarial attack frameworks specifically designed to test unlearning robustness, and standardized benchmarks for evaluating whether sensitive information has truly been removed. This positions unlearning as critical infrastructure for trustworthy AI deployment.
